data.title: Denmark Consumer Confidence Lowest Since 2023
data.description:
Denmark’s consumer confidence index declined to -20.1 in November 2025 from -19.5 in the previous month, marking its weakest reading since March 2023.

The decline reflected worsening sentiment about the country’s economic situation over the past 12 months (-34.4 vs -32.9 in October), as did their expectations for the next 12 months (-27.2 vs -25).

Households also reported a deterioration in their personal financial situation (-15.1 vs -14.3).

Meanwhile, consumers' expectations regarding the climate for major purchases at present remain unchanged at -19.5, while expectations for the coming year became slightly less pessimistic (-9.1 vs -9.6).

Among components not included in the overall confidence indicator, concerns about unemployment over the next 12 months continued to rise (18.6 vs 15.4), while the expectations for price developments in the coming year also increased (30.8 vs 29.6).

data.title: UN children's agency to move majority of jobs out of Geneva and New York following funding cuts
data.description:
By Olivia Le Poidevin and Crispian Balmer

The majority of staff jobs at the United Nations children's agency in Geneva and New York will be relocated to cheaper locations, as the agency faces a 20% funding reduction due to global cuts in foreign aid, UNICEF said late on Wednesday.

The United Nations Children's Fund will relocate at least 70% of its staff in the two cities to lower-cost duty stations to cut costs, it said in a statement.

Swiss newspaper Tribune de Geneve reported on Wednesday that about 300 jobs could be relocated away from UNICEF's regional office for Europe and Central Asia in Geneva.

UNICEF has described its plans to move jobs from New York - which remains its global headquarters - and Geneva to Rome, Budapest, Florence, Istanbul, Brussels and Valencia.

The agency and the Swiss foreign ministry would not confirm to Reuters how many jobs would be relocated and where they would go. The Swiss foreign ministry said about 100 jobs would stay in Geneva, which UNICEF said would be for global advocacy and coordination.

An Italian government source said jobs were being moved to Rome, but did not provide further details.

UN agencies face major cuts and a structural overhaul following a substantial reduction in foreign aid spending by the U.S. and major European donors.

UNICEF projects a 20% reduction in income over the next four years when compared to 2024 levels, prompting a 25% cut in headquarters and regional budgets and the relocation of staff to lower-cost locations.

In May, UNICEF said it would consolidate its regional offices for Europe and Central Asia and for the Middle East and North Africa into a single regional office based in Amman, Jordan.

UNICEF's relocations are the latest in a series of cuts and restructurings to UN agencies based in Geneva. On Tuesday, the World Health Organization said its workforce would shrink by nearly a quarter - or more than 2,000 jobs - by the middle of next year.

The International Labour Organization is considering abolishing up to 295 posts and relocating dozens of staff to cities like Turin as it faces "critical" cash-flow problems.

data.title: UK Stocks-Factors to watch on November 20
data.description:
Britain's FTSE 100 CURRENCYCOM:UK100 index is seen opening higher on Thursday, with futures up 0.47%.

* HALMA: Halma raised its annual revenue growth forecast, after posting a 29.3% rise in half-year adjusted pretax profit, thanks to strong US demand for its photonics products that are used to build data centres.

* JD SPORTS FASHION :JD Sports Fashion forecast full-year profit within the lower end of current market expectations, saying it was mindful of incrementally weaker macro and consumer indicators in recent weeks.

* ROLLS-ROYCE: Emirates will perform maintenance, repair and overhaul for the Trent 900 engines on its A380 aircraft from 2027, under a memorandum of understanding signed with engine maker Rolls-Royce , the airline said.

* DR MARTENS : Dr Martens reported a narrower first-half loss on Thursday, primarily supported by higher sales in the Americas, and maintained its fiscal 2026 forecast despite flagging the potential impact of U.S. tariffs.

* NATIONWIDE BUILDING SOCIETY : Nationwide Building Society reported a 46% rise in income for the first half of its financial year, as it increased lending and deposits while integrating Virgin Money, which it took over last year.

* JOHNSON MATTHEY : Johnson Matthey said its CFO Richard Pike will step down from the role, and will be replaced by Alastair Judge.

* SENIOR : Senior said it expects overall performance for the full year to be "comfortably ahead" of previous expectations, supported by strong growth in its Aerospace and Flexonics divisions.

* COMMODITIES: Oil prices edged up, gold prices edged lower, and copper rose on Thursday.

* EX-DIVS: National Grid , Vodafone Group , Scottish Mortgage Investment Trust PLC , DCC PLC will trade without entitlement to their latest dividend pay-out on Thursday.

* For more on the factors affecting European stocks, please click on:

TODAY'S UK PAPERS

data.title: Mitie Group's Pretax Profit Falls But Order Book Grows
data.description:
By Adam Whittaker

Mitie Group's pretax profit slipped but the company's revenue, order book and pipeline grew.

The U.K. outsourcing company said its profit before tax for the six months through Sept. 30 fell to 49.8 million pounds ($65 million) from 56.8 million pounds in the same period a year prior.

Revenue for the period rose 10% to 2.68 billion pounds on contract wins, pricing growth and contributions from acquisitions.

The group said it was confident in delivering its strategic plan through 2027, and that artificial intelligence was helping it redesign and automate workflows.

Over the period, it extended or renewed contracts valued at up to 3.8 billion pounds, marking a record six-month performance, it said.

Its interim dividend rose 8% to 1.4 pence a share.

Meanwhile, the company said it agreed to buy refrigeration maintenance services company Forest Group Holdings for up to 7 million pounds in cash. The deal comprises an initial payment of 4.5 million pounds plus performance-linked deferred payments of up to 2.5 million pounds over three years.

Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com

data.title: French Edenred adds Tesla chargers to its European electromobility network
data.description: French firm Edenred , which provides corporate benefits including payment cards and vouchers, said on Thursday it had partnered with Tesla to integrate the U.S.-based electric carmaker's Supercharger station network to its own fleet of charging products, including UTA eCharge and Ticket Fleet Pro.

data.title: Berenberg initiates Grenke with 'buy' as recovery gains traction
data.description:
** Berenberg initiates German leasing company Grenke at "buy", says its recovery is gaining traction and it's time for a re-rating

** Shares in Grenke are seen up 6.7% in early Frankfurt trade

** New business was muted from 2020 to 2022, but it has picked up since 2023, Berenberg says

** Net interest income is expected to grow strongly, as old, low-margin business is replaced and margins gain stability, it adds

** Grenke is trading close to historical lows, at a significant discount to its fair value, Berenberg says

** Positive earnings momentum should help the company regain investor trust and be revalued, the broker says, setting its PT at EUR 29, compared to Wednesday's close of EUR 13.04

data.title: Fed Rate Cut Still Expected, But Likely in January — Market Talk
data.description: The Federal Reserve remains in easing mode even though the latest FOMC minutes were on the hawkish side, Jefferies's Mohit Kumar says in a note. "Even if the Fed skips December, we expect them to ease in January," the global economist says. The Fed responds to data and Jefferies expects employment figures to carry a greater weight than inflation over the coming months. "Our base case remains one of a modest slowdown in employment with unemployment rate rising towards 4.5%," Kumar says. That scenario would be consistent with further easing from the Fed and taking rates toward neutral, he adds. The neutral policy rate is an estimated level which neither stimulates nor restricts the economy. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Novartis seen up 1-3% after growth targets announcement
data.description:
** Shares in Novartis are called opening 1-3% higher, with traders citing the Swiss drugmaker's currency-adjusted sales growth forecast of 5% to 6% until 2030

** Vontobel says mid-term targets came in higher than the broker had estimated, points to raised peak sales forecasts for cancer drugs Kisqali and Scemblix

** Given consistent strong execution of commitments by management in the past, "the new outlook offers potential for higher estimates," Vontobel says

** J.P.Morgan sees potential for upgrades to consensus numbers through 2029 and 2030 as a positive for the shares
